title: Embedding Whole Sentences
concept: Instead of separate word vectors, sentence embeddings compress an entire text into a single dense vector representing its overall meaning.

exercises.prompt: What does a sentence embedding represent in a single vector?

options.text: Only the first letter of each sentence

exercises.explain: Traditional models look at words individually. Sentence embeddings allow the AI to look at the entire sentence at once to capture its complete meaning.

cards.title: The Big Picture
cards.body: If we only look at words one by one, "not bad" and "bad" seem almost the same. But their meanings are totally opposite! To truly understand human language, AI needs a way to look at the entire sentence all at once. Let's see how it does that!

cards.title: What is a Sentence Embedding?
cards.body: A sentence embedding is a technique that takes a whole sentence and compresses it into a single list of numbers (a vector). This vector represents the overall meaning and context of the entire sentence, rather than just looking at the individual words on their own.

cards.title: The Sticky Note Summary
cards.body: Imagine reading a whole book and writing a short, one-sentence summary on a sticky note. That sticky note represents the entire story in a tiny space. Sentence embeddings do the same: they read an entire sentence and condense it into a single list of numbers that captures the main idea!

cards.title: Sentences in Action
cards.body: If you give an AI the sentence "I love warm sunny days", it doesn't just look up "love" or "sunny". It runs the entire sentence through a model to generate one vector, like [0.25, -0.81, 0.44]. This single vector captures the warm, happy sentiment of the whole phrase!

cards.title: More Than Just Adding Words
cards.body: Many people think sentence embeddings are just made by adding up the vectors of each individual word. But that's a myth! Modern AI models look at word order, grammar, and context to build a unique vector that captures how the words work together.

cards.title: Individual Words vs. Full Sentences
cards.body: See how looking at the whole sentence changes the AI's understanding compared to just looking at isolated words.

left.text: Looks at 'not' and 'bad' separately, missing the actual combined emotion.

right.text: Captures the entire phrase 'not bad' to understand it means 'good'.

exercises.prompt: How does a sentence embedding handle a 10-word sentence compared to word-by-word vectors?

options.text: It represents the entire sentence as a single vector.
It creates a separate vector for every single letter.
It translates the sentence into another language.
It deletes all the verbs to save computer memory.

exercises.explain: Traditional word vectors give you a list of vectors for each word. Sentence embeddings compress the whole sentence into just one vector.

exercises.prompt: True or False: "The dog chased the cat" and "The cat chased the dog" will have the exact same sentence embedding vector.
exercises.explain: Sentence embeddings capture the context and meaning of the entire sentence, so "The dog chased the cat" and "The cat chased the dog" will have different vectors because their meanings are different.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how sentence embeddings represent text.
exercises.sentence: Sentence embeddings compress an entire text into a single vector that represents its overall meaning.
exercises.explain: Sentence embeddings look at the whole picture to capture the overall meaning of a sentence, rather than just listing individual words.

exercises.prompt: Which explanation of sentence embeddings is correct?

options.text: AI: "By comparing sentence embeddings, we can group sentences by their meaning, even if they use different words."
AI: "Sentence embeddings work by counting the total number of vowels and matching sentences with the same count."
AI: "To embed a sentence, we simply multiply the letters together to find the alphabetical average."

exercises.explain: The first option is generated by an AI assistant because it accurately describes how sentence embeddings group texts by overall meaning, rather than just matching identical words.

exercises.prompt: If you convert 'The dog chased the cat' and 'A hound ran after the kitty' into sentence embeddings, what happens?

options.text: They will have completely opposite vectors because they share no words.
They will be represented by thousands of separate, unconnected word vectors.
They will have very similar vector representations because their overall meaning is the same.
The AI will crash because it cannot compare sentences without matching words.

exercises.explain: Sentence embeddings take the context of all words into account to create a single vector, meaning sentences with similar meanings get similar vectors, even if they share zero words!
